title: Ensure GKE clusters are not running using the Compute  Engine default service account

input_row.title: Ensure GKE clusters are not running using the Compute  Engine default service account
input_row.description: Create and use minimally privileged Service accounts to run GKE clusters instead of using the Compute Engine default Service account. Unnecessary permissions could be abused in the case of a node compromise.
input_row.rationale: A GCP service account (as distinct from a Kubernetes ServiceAccount) is an identity that an instance or an application can be used to run GCP API requests. This identity is used to identify virtual machine instances to other Google Cloud Platform services. By default, Kubernetes Engine nodes use the Compute Engine default service account. This account has broad access by default, as defined by access scopes, making it useful to a wide variety of applications on the VM, but it has more permissions than are required to run your Kubernetes Engine cluster. A minimally privileged service account should be created and used to run the Kubernetes Engine cluster instead of using the Compute Engine default service account, and create separate service accounts for each Kubernetes Workload (See recommendation 5.2.2). Kubernetes Engine requires, at a minimum, the node service account to have the monitoring.viewer, monitoring.metricWriter, and logging.logWriter roles. Additional roles may need to be added for the nodes to pull images from GCR.
input_row.audit: Using Google Cloud Console: Internal Only - General 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list 2. Select the cluster under test and under Security ensure Service account is not set to default. To check the permissions allocated to the service account are the minimum required for cluster operation: 1. Go to IAM by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/iam 2. From the list of Service accounts, ensure each cluster Service account has only the following roles: • Logs Writer • Monitoring Metric Writer • Monitoring Viewer Using Command line: To check which Service account is set for an existing cluster, run the following command: gcloud container clusters describe $CLUSTER_NAME --zone $COMPUTE_ZONE -- format json | jq '.nodeConfig.serviceAccount' The output of the above command will return default if default Service account is used for Project access. To check that the permissions allocated to the service account are the minimum required for cluster operation: gcloud projects get-iam-policy \ --flatten="bindings[].members" \ --format='table(bindings.role)' \ --filter="bindings.members:" Review the output to ensure that the service account only has the roles required to run the cluster: • roles/logging.logWriter • roles/monitoring.metricWriter • roles/monitoring.viewer
input_row.remediation: Using Google Cloud Console: To create a minimally privileged service account: 1. Go to Service Accounts by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/iam- admin/serviceaccounts. 2. Click on CREATE SERVICE ACCOUNT. 3. Enter Service Account Details. Internal Only - General 4. Click CREATE AND CONTINUE. 5. Within Service Account permissions add the following roles: o Logs Writer. o Monitoring Metric Writer. o `Monitoring Viewer. 6. Click CONTINUE. 7. Grant users access to this service account and create keys as required. 8. Click DONE. Note: A new cluster will need to be created specifying the minimally privileged service account, and workloads will need to be migrated to the new cluster and the old cluster deleted. Using Command Line: To create a minimally privileged service account: gcloud iam service-accounts create --display-name "GKE Node Service Account" export NODE_SA_EMAIL=gcloud iam service-accounts list --format='value(email)' --filter='displayName:GKE Node Service Account' Grant the following roles to the service account: export PROJECT_ID=gcloud config get-value project gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding --member serviceAccount: --role roles/monitoring.metricWriter gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding --member serviceAccount: --role roles/monitoring.viewer gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding --member serviceAccount: --role roles/logging.logWriter Note: A new cluster will need to be created specifying the minimally privileged service account, and workloads will need to be migrated to the new cluster and the old cluster deleted.
input_row.impact: Instances are automatically granted the https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud- platform scope to allow full access to all Google Cloud APIs. This is so that the IAM permissions of the instance are completely determined by the IAM roles of the Service account. Thus if Kubernetes workloads were using cluster access scopes to perform actions using Google APIs, they may no longer be able to, if not permitted by the permissions of the Service account. To remediate, follow recommendation 5.2.2. The Service account roles listed here are the minimum required to run the cluster. Additional roles may be required to pull from a private instance of Google Container Registry (GCR).
input_row.default_value: By default, nodes use the Compute Engine default service account when you create a new cluster.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The audit approach will be automated because:
- GCP APIs exist to query the service account configuration for GKE clusters.
- The audit mentions specific Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands that can check compliance and retrieve the necessary properties.
- No human judgment is required as all verifiable parameters can be checked programmatically.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_gke_cluster_service_account_minimally_privileged

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:** 
The automated program would check the following:
- Use the command `gcloud container clusters describe $CLUSTER_NAME --zone $COMPUTE_ZONE --format json` to retrieve the service account associated with the GKE cluster's node configuration.
- Validate the returned service account against the default service account identifier (`default`) to determine if a minimally privileged service account is being used.
- To check the IAM roles assigned to the service account, use:
  ```bash
  gcloud projects get-iam-policy $PROJECT_ID --flatten="bindings[].members" --format='table(bindings.role)' --filter="bindings.members:serviceAccount:$SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL"
  ```
  where `$SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL` is the email address of the service account being checked.
- Ensure that the roles returned are limited to:
  - `roles/logging.logWriter`
  - `roles/monitoring.metricWriter`
  - `roles/monitoring.viewer`
  
Clear Pass/Fail criteria:
- Pass: The service account is not the default service account and has only the minimum required roles listed above.
- Fail: The service account is the default service account or has more roles than required.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as automated is correct. The compliance check can be performed using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) without requiring manual intervention. The necessary information about the service account used by GKE clusters can be retrieved programmatically, making automation feasible and efficient.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly technically correct. The `gcloud container clusters describe` command is appropriate for retrieving the service account associated with a GKE cluster. The use of `gcloud projects get-iam-policy` to check IAM roles is also valid.
- However, the instructions could be more explicit about handling multiple clusters and zones, and the potential need to iterate over clusters in a project.
- The program name `gcp_gke_cluster_service_account_minimally_privileged` is descriptive but could be more concise.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Handling Multiple Clusters:** The assessment should consider scenarios where multiple clusters exist within a project. A loop or script to iterate through all clusters would enhance the automation process.
- **Program Name Suggestion:** A more concise and clear program name could be `gcp_gke_service_account_privilege_check`.
- **Role Verification:** The list of roles should be verified against the latest best practices, as additional roles might be required based on specific use cases or updates in GCP's IAM policies.
- **Error Handling:** Include error handling in the script to manage cases where the service account or roles cannot be retrieved.
- **Documentation:** Provide documentation or comments within the script to explain each step and its purpose, which aids in maintaining and updating the script.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded, and the use of GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK is appropriate for this compliance check. The technical details are mostly accurate, and the approach aligns with GCP security best practices.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Modify:** Implement the suggested improvements for handling multiple clusters, refining the program name, and enhancing documentation and error handling. These modifications will improve the robustness and clarity of the automated compliance check.
